coredata.dc:title: Sensitive acid phosphatase assay based on light-activated specific oxidase mimic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Xiaohong
Li, Hanmei
Ma, Huijun
Zhang, Han
Jin, Lihua
coredata.dc:description: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is a key marker in the diagnosis of many diseases. However, exploiting a simple and sensitive sensor for the real-time quantitative analysis of ACP is still challenging. Herein, we attempted to develop a sensitive colorimetric sensing strategy for the detection of ACP based on light-activated oxidase mimic property of carbon dots (CDs). The synthesized CDs were proved to be capable of intrinsic light-activated oxidase mimic activity, which could generate reactive oxygen species to oxidize chromogenic substrate under ultraviolet light stimulation. Interestingly, this light-activated oxidase mimic behavior would be effectively suppressed by the antioxidant ascorbic acid (AA), a product from the hydrolysis of 2-phospho-L-ascorbic acid trisodium (AAP) mediated by ACP. Based on the above property, a facile and sensitive colorimetric sensing method for ACP was developed. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range for ACP 0.1–5.5 U/L, and the detection limit was 0.056 U/L. Compared with conventional nanozyme based ACP assay systems, the catalytic activity of light-activated nanozyme could be conveniently regulated by switching the light on and off, which made it easier to precisely control the extent of the reaction and ensured the accuracy of the assay. In addition, the proposed sensing system would be readout directly by the naked eye or smartphone-based RGB analysis system, and have been successfully applied to analyze diluted in diluted fetal bovine serum and urine samples spiked with ACP. All these results indicated that this approach holds good promise for future applications in clinical analysis and point-of-care (POC) biosensor platforms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxidase mimic
Light-activated
Acid phosphatase
Colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Verification of light-controllable oxidation mimic enzyme activity
Steady state dynamic analysis and specific activity measurement
Determination of the inhibitors of ACP
Characterization and enzyme-like activities of CDs
The mechanism investigation of enzyme-like activities of CDs-4
Quantitative detection of ascorbic acid and acid phosphatase

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2019YFD1002400 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University ( 300102220513 ) and Tang scholar.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of prepared CDs; (λex = 365 nm, λem = 444 nm); (B) TEM image of CDs-4; (C) the light controlled oxidase mimic activity of prepared CDs-4 with or without the action of 365 nm UV light with 10 min.
(A–E) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s for prepared CDs; (F) variation in the content of nitrogen-doped types for prepared CDs (a-pyridinic N, b-pyrrolic N, c-graphitic N).

(A) The effect of molecular oxygen on the light controlled oxidase mimic activity of CDs-4. (B)The effect of several free radical scavengers on the oxidation of TMB by CDs-4; EPR characterization of ROS generated by CDs-4 light stimulation: (C) DMPO for •O2 − (in methanol); (D) TEMP for 1O2; (E) DMPO for •OH (in water).

(A) The absorption spectra of TMB-CDs-4 sensor system with or without AA or ACP; (B) The absorption spectra of TMB-CDs-4 system with different concentrations of AA; (C) The linear calibration plots for concentrations of AA; (D) The absorption spectra of CDs-4 +TMB system with different concentrations of ACP; (E) The linear calibration plots for concentrations of ACP; (F) The images of the reaction system after incubation with different concentrations of ACP, the numbers on the images are the corresponding ACP concentration (U/L); (G) The RGB value of the system with different concentrations of ACP incubation; (H) The linear relationship between the grayscale values (calculated from the average values of red, blue and green) and the concentration of ACP; (I) Selectivity analysis for ACP sensing system(ACP 4 U/L, ALP 20 U/L and other enzymes 200 U/L).. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Relationship between catalytic stability and storage time of CDs-4. (B) Changes in oxidase mimic catalysis behavior of CDs-4 under alternating UV light irradiation. (C) Effects of CDs-4 on L929 cell viability.
Schematic diagram of the colorimetric detection of ACP based on CDs light-controllable oxidation mimic enzyme.
Recovery experiment of ACP in biological samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive acid phosphatase assay based on light-activated specific oxidase mimic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is a key marker in the diagnosis of many diseases. However, exploiting a simple and sensitive sensor for the real-time quantitative analysis of ACP is still challenging. Herein, we attempted to develop a sensitive colorimetric sensing strategy for the detection of ACP based on light-activated oxidase mimic property of carbon dots (CDs). The synthesized CDs were proved to be capable of intrinsic light-activated oxidase mimic activity, which could generate reactive oxygen species to oxidize chromogenic substrate under ultraviolet light stimulation. Interestingly, this light-activated oxidase mimic behavior would be effectively suppressed by the antioxidant ascorbic acid (AA), a product from the hydrolysis of 2-phospho-L-ascorbic acid trisodium (AAP) mediated by ACP. Based on the above property, a facile and sensitive colorimetric sensing method for ACP was developed. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range for ACP 0.1–5.5 U/L, and the detection limit was 0.056 U/L. Compared with conventional nanozyme based ACP assay systems, the catalytic activity of light-activated nanozyme could be conveniently regulated by switching the light on and off, which made it easier to precisely control the extent of the reaction and ensured the accuracy of the assay. In addition, the proposed sensing system would be readout directly by the naked eye or smartphone-based RGB analysis system, and have been successfully applied to analyze diluted in diluted fetal bovine serum and urine samples spiked with ACP. All these results indicated that this approach holds good promise for future applications in clinical analysis and point-of-care (POC) biosensor platforms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is an essential enzyme that maintains lysosomal metabolism in living organisms [1]. Under weakly acidic conditions, it plays a key role in the conversion of orthophosphate into containing inorganic phosphate and hydroxyl containing molecules [2]. Abnormal levels of ACP are closely associated with several diseases, such as prostate cancer [3], malignant bone tumor [1], Gaucher's disease [4] and hyperparathyroidism [5]. Therefore, the development of ACP assay is of great importance for the prognostic and diagnosis of these diseases. Up to now, several methods have been proposed for the detection of ACP, for example, electrochemical analysis [6], colorimetry [7–9], fluorescence [10], high performance liquid chromatography(HPLC) [11], and plasma resonance energy transfer(PRET) [12]. Although the above methods provided effective strategies for the detection of ACP, most of them require expensive and complex equipment or tedious sample pretreatment. Therefore, exploiting a simple and sensitive sensor for the quantitative analysis of ACP still remains a challenge.

Colorimetric assay is one of the most common tests used in the field of clinical diagnosis because of its simplicity, visibility and convenience [13]. It has been widely used for the detection of many tumor markers such as proteins, DNA, and small molecules [14–16]. However, most of the current colorimetric sensors were constructed based on enzymatic reactions, and a large proportion of enzymes used in these enzymatic reactions were natural enzymes, which suffered from a series of inherent limitations, such as high cost, poor stability and perishability. In recent years, the development of nanozyme material opens up new approaches for the construction of colorimetric sensors. They possess similar catalytic activity to natural enzymes, but with better stability and lower cost [17,18]. So far, several kinds of nanomaterials including metal oxide materials [19–22], precious metal materials [23–25], and carbon nanomaterials [26–28] have been proved to be equipped with enzyme-like catalytic activity.

Among the various nanomaterials that make up nanozymes, carbon dots (CDs) have been attracted tremendous attentions due to their series of brilliant merits: ultra-small size, unique elemental composition, good biocompatibility, as well as considerable catalytic properties [29,30]. As the research progresses, it was found that CDs could be used to successfully mimic the function of multiple natural enzymes, like oxidase [31,32], peroxidase [33,34], catalase [35,36] and superoxide dismutase [37,38]. Interestingly, some of their oxidase-like activity could also be regulated by the light stimulation. Compared to ordinary nanozymes, taking advantage of the light to control the oxidase-like activity of CDs was simpler, faster and more attractive. Their catalytic activity could be regulated conveniently by controlling the light intensity irradiation and time, which made them easier precisely control extent of reaction towards the chromogenic indicator and ensured the accuracy of the assay, as well as making CDs promising for a wide range of applications in smart devices and point-of-care (POC) treatments [39]. To date, researchers have designed a series of light-activated CDs nanozymes, and have successfully applied them to the field of photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy [31], biosensors [40], and removal of organic pollutants [32,41]. However, the present studies based on the application of light-activated nanozymes were still relatively rare. Exploring various CDs materials with efficient light response activity and expanding their applications remain an important research direction for the future.

In this work, we expanded the application of the light-controllable oxidase mimic behavior of CDs for assay ACP activity for the first time. As shown in Scheme 1 , the prepared CDs possessed an intrinsic UV light-activated oxidase -like activity, which could generate reactive oxygen species (•O− 2 and 1O2) for oxidizing colorless substrates-3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine(TMB) to blue products-oxidized TMB (oxTMB). However, the catalytic activity of CDs could be suppressed by ascorbic acid (AA), an antioxidant producing from the ACP-mediated hydrolysis of 2-phospho-L-ascorbic acid (AAP), thus leading to a decrease in the generation of oxTMB. This result was accompanied by obvious changes in absorption and visible color, which could be readout directly by the naked eye or smartphone-based RGB analysis system. Based on the above properties, a facile, light-switchable and low-cost colorimetric sensing strategy for ACP was developed. Compared with the previous colorimetric detection of ACP by nanozymes, the greatest advantage of the proposed method was that the oxidation state of the chromogenic indicator TMB would be precisely controlled by the modulation of the light, which ensured the reproducibility and operability of the detection method.

AA, TMB, AAP, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), EDTA disodium salt, L-tryptophan and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-1-nitrogen oxide (DMPO) was purchased from Dojindo. Co. Ethylenediamine, citric acid, acetic acid (HAC), Sodium acetate (NaAC) and other salt reagents were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. ACP (from potato, ≥0.5 unit per mg solid) and other enzymes were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Tissue and Blood Acid phosphatase Activity Detection Kit was bought form Beijing Solaibao Technology Co.

The 365 nm ultraviolet light stimulation was produced from Hand-hold UV Lamps (Jingke, Shanghai). A UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the UV–vis spectrum. RF-5031 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to record fluorescence spectra. A Fourier infrared spectrometer (Bruker, USA) was used to obtain the infrared spectrum of the CDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed in Talos F200X (Thermo Fisher). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of CDs was obtained on an Axis Ultra X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK). The X-ray powder diffraction spectrum (XRD) of CDs was obtained on the D8 Advance X-ray powder analyzer (Bruker, USA). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was performed on Bruker E500–9.5/12.

The CDs were prepared as the following procedure: ethylenediamine (0.025 mol) and citric acid (0.025 mol) were dissolved in 50 mL distilled water according with the previous literature [42]. Then, the reaction system was adjusted to five different pH conditions (pH = 4, pH = 6, pH = 8, pH = 10 and pH = 12) with NaOH solution, and the synthesized CDs were named as CDs-4, CDs-6, CDs-8, CDs-10 and CDs-12, respectively. These solutions were transferred to an autoclave (100 mL) lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and hydrothermally heated at 240 °C for 5 h. After that, the resulting products were further purified by the wet packed column chromatography: 200–300 mesh silica gel powder was mixed with distilled water and poured into a 30 cm column (column length of 10–15 cm). Then adding 1 mL of the reacted solution and keeping eluting with distilled water until the color of the unfolded agent under the drop was a clear solution. The final CDs powder was obtained after rotary evaporation and freeze-drying operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of light-controllable oxidation mimic enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: The light-activated oxidation mimic enzyme activities of CDs-4 were conducted as follows: 50 μL TMB (36 mM) and 100 μL CDs-4 (25 μg/mL) were mixed in 2850 μL HAC-NaAC buffer (50 mM, pH 4.8). Then the mixture was exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light stimulation in a fixed distance (3.2 cm) with an average intensity of 2.1 mW/cm2 for 10 min and its absorption spectra were measured at 500–800 nm. The relative control experiments were performed in the same condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state dynamic analysis and specific activity measurement
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect the affinity of CDs-4 with the substrate TMB, 25 μg/mL CDs-4 and different concentrations of TMB (0.1 mM–0.9 mM) were added to NaAc-HAc buffer (50 mM, pH 4.8). After mixing, the kinetic spectrum of the reaction system was monitored within 10 min under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. The enzyme parameters v max (the maximum reaction rate) and K m (the Michaelis−Menten constant) can be obtained from the Lineweaver-Burk diagram. The specific activity (SA) of CDs-4, defined as units of activity per milligram of nanozyme, was evaluated according to the protocol of previous reference [43].

50 μL 36 mM TMB was added into the solution containing 25 μg/mL CDs-4 and different concentrations of AA. The final volume was adjusted by 50 mM pH 4.8 HAc-NaAC buffer solution to 3.0 mL. The absorption spectra was recorded after irradiation under a 365 nm hand-hold UV lamp for 10 min.

For ACP determination, firstly, 10 mM monophosphate (AAP) and different concentrations of ACP (0–5 U/L) were added to HAc-NaAC buffer solution. After incubating at 37 °C for 30 min, the reaction solution was then added to another HAC-NaAc buffer solution containing 25 μg/mL CDs-4 and 600 μM TMB. The absorption spectra was recorded after irradiation under a 365 nm hand-hold UV lamp for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the inhibitors of ACP
ce:section.ce:para:
For inhibitors of ACP determination，different concentrations of NaF or Na2MO4 were mixed with 500 μL solution containing 10 mM AAP and 2.5 U/L ACP. After incubating at 37 °C for 30 min, the reaction solution was then added to another HAC-NaAc buffer solution containing 25 μg/mL CDs-4 and 600 μM TMB. Then, the absorption spectra was measured after irradiation under a 365 nm hand-hold UV lamp for 10 min. The inhibition efficiency of NaF or Na2MO4 was calculated according to the following formula:
Inhibiton
efficiency =
A inhibitor
−
A
no
inhibitor
A 0
−
A
no
inhibitor
× 100 %

Ainhibitor is A652 nm in the reaction solution with ACP and NaF/Na2MO4, Ano inhibitor is A652 nm in the reaction solution with ACP but without NaF or Na2MO4, and A0 is A652 nm in the reaction solution without ACP and NaF or Na2MO4.

We selected fresh fetal bovine serum as one of the actual biological samples for the measurement of ACP activity using the standard addition method. Fresh fetal bovine serum was centrifuged with 30 kDa Amicon cell for 30 min at 3000 rpm, then the filtrate was obtained for ACP analysis. Different concentrations of 50 μL ACP were added to a mixture containing 350 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 4.8, 50 mM), 50 μL 10 mM AAP and 50 μL filtrate of fetal bovine serum and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. After that, adding another 2350 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 4.8, 50 mM), 100 μL CDs-4 solution, 50 μL TMB (600 μM) to the above solution and react for 10 min under a 365 nm portable UV lamp.

We collected urine from a healthy volunteer for the experiment. The urine was firstly centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was extracted and then filtered through a 0.22 M filter membrane. Different concentrations of 50 μL ACP were added to a mixture containing 350 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 4.8, 50 mM), 50 μL 10 mM AAP and 50 μL treated urine sample and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. After that, adding another 2350 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 4.8, 50 mM), 100 μL CDs-4 solution, 50 μL TMB (600 μM) to the above solution. The absorption spectra were recorded after irradiation for 10 min under a 365 nm portable UV lamp.

The Tissue and Blood Acid Phosphatase (ACP) Kit reaction procedure was performed in strict accordance with the reagent instructions. ACP was not detected in the diluted fetal bovine serum and urine samples.

The cytotoxicity of the synthesized CDs-4 was measured using the CCK8 method. The mouse fibroblast cell line L929 were chosen as the cell model. Conventional Dulbecco's Modified Eagle (DMEM) medium containing approximately 1ⅹ104 cells were added to 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h in an incubator at an environment of 37 °C with 5% CO2. After that, fresh media containing different concentrations of CDs-4 were replaced. After incubated for another 24 h, the cytotoxicity was assessed using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) based on absorbance at 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and enzyme-like activities of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of CDs at different initial pH conditions were prepared by a hydrothermal method that was similar to previous literature [39]. As shown in Fig. 1 A, all of prepared CDs were equipped with fluorescence character with an optimal emission wavelength at 444 nm upon exciting at 365 nm. For their absorption spectra, two obvious absorption bands of located at 220–240 nm and 320–340 nm could be obtained, which were assigned to the typical absorption bands of π-π* transition and n-π* transition, respectively. The TEM images revealed that the prepared CDs possessed uniformly dispersed spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 3–5 nm (Fig. 1B and Fig. S1), which further confirmed the successful preparation of CDs.

In addition to the conventional fluorescence properties, the prepared CDs would also produce reactive oxygen species upon stimulation with 365 nm light and exhibit light controlled oxidase mimic activity. To verify this catalytic performance of CDs, we used TMB as a chromogenic probe due to its ability to be oxidized by the oxidase-mimetic activity of CDs to produce a blue product. As revealed in Fig. 1C and Fig. S2, all of the prepared CDs were equipped with certain light controlled oxidase mimic activity after exposing to 365 nm ultraviolet light stimulation for 10 min, which was due to the generation of oxTMB with a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm. As a control, the solution of single TMB or without light stimulation did not show a significant absorbance change. Meanwhile, the comparison revealed that this catalytic performance was decreased with the increasing of preparation pH conditions (Fig. S2) and did not show a positive correlation with the fluorescence properties of CDs (Table S1). Under the same conditions, CDs-4 exhibited the optimal light controlled oxidase mimic catalytic performance.

According to previous literature reports [32,41], the light controlled oxidase mimic catalytic performance of N-doped C materials was closely related to the content and form of N element in the carbon material. Therefore, FTIR spectra and XPS assay of CDs were further performed to analyze their surface chemical functional groups and elemental compositions. Fig. S3 displayed the detail FTIR analysis of CDs, and the existence of N forms could be identified from the infrared spectral data of N–H bonds. The peaks at 3430 cm−1-2945 cm−1 could be ascribed to the contribution of the stretching vibration of N–H, O–H and C–H, while the peaks at 1658 cm−1, 1565 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1were ascribed to the stretching vibration of the CO bond, bending vibrations of N–H and C–N = bonds, respectively.

For the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s, all of the prepared CDs could be fitted to three peaks designated as pyridinic N (399.4 eV), pyrrolic N (400.1 eV) and graphitic N (401.1 eV) [44,45], respectively (Fig. 2 A–E), while the N content of CDs-4 was the highest. It was interesting to note that the trend of the oxidase mimic catalytic activity of CDs was consistent with the variation of the total content of both pyrrolic N and graphitic N (Fig. 2F). According to previous reports [41], pyrrolic N was the main adsorption site for oxygen. The increase of its content could shorten the distance between oxygen and CDs, thus enhancing their light activated catalytic activity. Meanwhile, graphite N could triplet molecular oxygen into singlet oxygen. Therefore, the change of its content also led to the variation of light controlled oxidase mimic performance of CDs. Moreover, the variation in the type of N element in CDs could also be demonstrated from the UV–Vis absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 1A, the broad absorption peak near 300–400 nm for the same concentration of CDs gradually decreased and experienced a blue shift with increasing of preparation pH. According to previous literature [46], the improvement of light absorption was a promising strategy to increase reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. The absorption peak of CDs-4 was the most intense at the same mass concentration, indicating that it possessed the strongest light absorption ability. This blue shift in the absorption spectra of the CDs was attributed to the increase in pyridinic N in the system, which agreed well with the changes of pyridinic N derived from XPS surveys.

In addition, we also systematically studied the performance of light activated enzyme-like activities of CDs with CDs-4 as the representative. As we all known, the light activated oxidase mimic performance was also dependent upon many factors, such as solution pH, light stimulation time, substrate concentration and catalyst concentration. Therefore, we firstly investigated oxidase mimic performance of CDs-4 in pH range of 2–6, light stimulation time changing from 0 to 30 min and different substrate and catalyst concentration. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the absorbance achieved maximum value at pH 4, while the reaction velocity increased with the increasing light stimulation time, substrate concentration and catalyst concentration. Considering the sensitivity and relative error of the method and future biological sample application, pH 4.8 reaction condition, light stimulation time of 10 min, 25 μg/mL of CDs-4 and 600 μM of TMB were finally selected for following experiments. In addition to this, we also investigated the effect of the wavelength of light on the light activated oxidase mimic performance of CDs-4. The comparison experiments showed that the CDs-4 did not exhibit the light activated oxidase mimic property under the irradiation of the same light intensity from 450 nm visible light.

Moreover, we also investigated the steady-state parameters of CDs-4 according to the enzyme kinetics theory. The kinetic curve and double reciprocal curve were obtained by changing the TMB concentration and recording the absorbance changes at different reaction times. As shown in Fig. S5, the initial rate of TMB catalyzed by CDs gradually increased and then leveled off with the increase of TMB concentration. The trend was in accordance with the typical Michaelis-Menten curve, thus demonstrating that the whole catalytic process followed Michaelis-Menten behavior. The steady-state kinetic parameters K m and maximum initial rate v m were calculated as 0.25 mM and 3.04 × 10−8 M/s, respectively. The results were comparable to other values reported in the previous literatures (Table S2) and demonstrate that this CDs −4 had a good affinity for TMB. The further specific activity (SA) investigation (Figs. S5C–D) demonstrated that the SA of CD-4 was 1.38 U/mg, which suggested that CDs-4 had good light-controllable oxidase mimic activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism investigation of enzyme-like activities of CDs-4
ce:section.ce:para: As previous literature reported [39], this light activated oxidase mimic performance was due to the action of reactive oxygen species, for which we have performed a series of control experiments to verify. Fig. 3 A revealed the important role of molecular oxygen in this light activated catalytic process. As shown, the TMB-CDs-4 system had a distinct absorption peak at 652 nm under light irradiation in an environment of air and O2, while the absorption peak at 652 nm disappeared in an environment saturated with N2, indicating that molecular oxygen was indispensable in the catalytic reduction reaction. In addition, we have done a series of experiments to verify the type of reactive oxygen species, for example, using p-BQ to verify superoxide radicals, using isopropyl alcohol to verify hydroxyl radicals, using L-tryptophan to verify singlet oxygen, and using EDTA disodium salt as an electron trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 3B, only the system with the addition of p-benzoquinone and L-tryptophan showed significant decreases of the absorption compared to the other reagents, which showed that the singlet oxygen was the main intermediate of this light controlled process. To further verify the type of active intermediates, the EPR spectra was further performed (Fig. 3C–E). When DMPO and TEMP were used as trapping agents, it can be observed two distinct sets of 1:1:1:1 signal peaks and 1:1:1 signal peaks attributable to •O− 2 and 1O2, respectively. In addition, a weak signal peak of 1:2:2:1 can also be obtained with DMPO as a trapping agent, which indicated that a small amount of ∙OH was also generated during the catalytic process. Therefore, •O− 2 and 1O2 were recognized as the main reactive oxygen species produced light stimulation behavior of CDs-4, which dominated the overall light controlled oxidase mimic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of ascorbic acid and acid phosphatase
ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is a common reductant and an essential biomolecule for the physiological metabolic processes of living organisms. The presence of AA would inhibit the activity of the light activated oxidation-mimicking enzymes of CDs (Fig. 4 A); therefore, we established a simple colorimetric strategy for the quantitative analysis of AA. Fig. 4B displayed the absorbance change of TMB-CDs system before and after adding AA. It could be found that the absorbance decreased significantly after the addition of AA, and a good linear relationship between the absorbance and the concentration of AA (0.5 μM–50 μM) in a certain concentration range would be obtained (Fig. 4C). The detection limit was 0.2 μM (S/N = 3), which was comparable to the results reported in previous literature [47,48]. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for seven repeated times and was calculated to be 1.5%, which indicated that the method had good reproducibility in the detection of AA.

Since ACP could catalyze the hydrolysis of AAP under acidic conditions to produce a certain amount of AA and inorganic phosphate, the established TMB-CDs-4 system would also be used for the quantitative colorimetric identification of ACP (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4D showed the variation of the absorption spectrum with different concentrations of ACP. It could be seen that the absorbance gradually decreased with the increasing of ACP concentration, and the absorbance and ACP concentration showed a good linearity in the range of 0.1–5.5 U/L (Fig. 4E). The detection limit of this method was low (0.056 U/L) and the analytical performance was comparable to previously reported methods, as shown in Table S3. The RSD for seven replicated experiments was calculated to be 2.7%, which indicated that the proposed method for ACP detection was sensitive and stable.

In addition, we also tried to establish the relationship between ACP concentration and the RGB (red, yellow and blue) output values from the images information captured by smartphones in order to expand the application of the present method for POC detection. Fig. 4F revealed the images of the reaction solution corresponding to different concentrations of ACP taken by Huawei nova5 Pro smartphone. As can be seen from the figure, although it was difficult to distinguish similar colors at low concentrations with the naked eye alone, the corresponding RGB output values were indeed different for each image. Subsequently, we compared and analyzed the RGB output values of each image by GetRGB software and found that the R value increased as the ACP concentration decreased, while the other two values did not change significantly (Fig. 4G). A good linear relationship between the grayscale values (calculated from the average values of red, blue and green) and the concentration of ACP in range of 0.5–5.5 U/L would be obtained (Fig. 4H). The result was in general agreement with that detected result by the UV–vis spectrophotometer, which demonstrated the application prospects of the method in POC detection.

In order to study the anti-interference ability and selectivity of the above CDs-based colorimetric sensors, we evaluated the influence of some biologically relevant metal ions, small molecular substances and common enzymes on the established method. As shown in Fig. 4I, in contrast to the addition of other common enzymes, only ACP could cause a significant decrease in the absorbance of the system, proving that the method had good enzyme selectivity. For the anti-interference experiment, it would be found that the presence of common ions and amino acids did not have a significant effect on the response performance of the method (Fig. S6), which revealed the high anti-interference of this ACP assay method. Moreover, the performance of the CDs-4 used in this method was very stable. The comparison experiments revealed no significant change in the light activated oxidase mimic activity of the CDs-4 within 100 days (Fig. 5 A).The oxidase mimic activity of CDs-4 was sensitive to the light activation operation, and real-time regulation of CDs-4 oxidase mimic activity can be achieved by simply switching UV light (Fig. 5B). Based on the above results, this CDs-4 based colorimetric method was promising as a new candidate for the quantitative detection of ACP. Moreover, the cytotoxicity assay showed that the L929 cells proliferated normally and did not show significant apoptosis after co-incubation with different concentrations of CDs-4 for 24 h (Fig. 5C), indicating that the prepared CDs had good cytocompatibility.

And beyond that, we further extended the application of the established method to the colorimetric screening of ACP activity inhibitors. The experimental results showed that the presence of either NaF or Na2MoO4 inhibited the activity of ACP. The absorbance of the system gradually recovered with the increase of NaF or Na2MoO4 concentration (Fig. S7). The maximum inhibition of ACP by NaF and Na2MO4 was 93.65% and 91.88%, respectively. The IC50 of NaF and Na2MO4 on ACP were calculated to be 0.058 mM and 0.082 mM, respectively. These results indicated that both NaF and Na2MO4 had good inhibition efficiency on ACP activity.

To evaluate the practicability of our established ACP colorimetric sensor, the standard addition method was chosen to determine the ACP content in diluted fetal bovine serum and urine sample. As shown in Table 1 , ACP was not detected in the diluted fetal bovine serum and urine samples. For diluted fetal bovine serum sample, the recoveries ranged from 96.6% to 98.0%, and the RSDs of five replicate measurements were 1.5%–4.2%. While for diluted urine sample, the recoveries ranged from 98.5% to 100.2% with RSD between 0.7% and 1.8%. The ACP levels detected by the proposed sensing system were in general agreement with the results of commercially available kits (disodium phenyl phosphate method). The average of the two methods was further analyzed by t-test and it was found that there was a significant difference between the two methods. All these results demonstrated that the CDs-4 based colorimetric assay had good accuracy in practical applications and held the potential to be used as an alternative analytical method for the detection of ACP in biological samples.

In summary, a convenient and sensitive colorimetric strategy for the sensing of ACP was developed based on the light-activated oxidation mimic behavior of CDs. The CDs-4 was prepared by a simple method (hydrothermal synthesis) and exhibited brilliant oxidation mimic performance under UV light stimulation. The mechanism studies demonstrated that the generation of reactive oxygen species was the main contributor in their light-activated oxidase mimic performance, while the doping form of nitrogen atoms in their composition might dominate their intrinsic light-activated oxidase-like property. Because antioxidants could interfere with this photochemical process of CDs-4, it was possible to quantify ACP by the action of AA generated by ACP-mediated hydrolysis reactions. A good linearity between absorbance and the ACP concentration in the range of 0.1–5.5 U/L was obtained with a detection limit of 0.056 U/L. Compared with conventional detection methods, this method detection process would be simply controlled by a portable UV lamp, which had a wide range of application prospects in the field of POC detection. In addition, the present sensing method could also be successfully applied to the detection of ACP in diluted fetal bovine serum and urine sample, which provided a broad prospect for carbon materials in the field of biology and photocatalysis.

Xiaohong Qiao: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft.Hanmei Li: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft.Huijun Ma: Material Characterization, Formal analysis.Han Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis.Lihua Jin: Project administration, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2019YFD1002400), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (300102220513) and Tang scholar.
